[PAPER COMPLETO — 37002 caracteres, 7 páginas]

Evaluating Pose Estimation as a Solution
to the Fall Detection Problem
Ygor Rebouc¸as Serpa, Matheus Batista Nogueira, Pedro Paulo Macˆedo Neto and Maria Andr´eia Formico Rodrigues
Programa de P´os-Graduac¸˜ao em Inform´atica Aplicada (PPGIA)
Universidade de Fortaleza (UNIFOR)
Fortaleza-CE, Brazil
{ygor.reboucas, matb369, pedroppmn, andreia.formico}@gmail.com
Abstract—In this age of evolving technological capabilities,
assisted living has proven useful to ease the frailty that comes
with aging. Within this context, the detection and prevention of
accidents are paramount to ensure a longer life expectancy for the
elderly. Over the years, many approaches for fall detection have
been proposed, such as ambient sensors, wearable devices, and
automated camera monitoring. A recent approach is to use pose
estimation software to identify humans and pinpoint the location
of their most important joints. This pose information can be
later used as features for an effective fall detection system. This
scenario begs the question: Can pose estimation methods be as
effective as the sensor or other camera-based ones? To answer
this question, we analyzed three pose estimation frameworks,
totalizing eleven models, paired with a simple neural network
classiﬁer. In our experiments, we have obtained competitive
results among the state-of-the-art on the UR Fall Detection
dataset, a multi-modal fall detection benchmark, comprised of
RGB, depth, and acceleration data. More speciﬁcally, our best
model achieved a sensitivity rate of 94.5% and a speciﬁcity rate
of 99.9%, in line with the best camera and sensor-based solutions.
Index Terms—fall detection, neural networks, pose estimation,
evaluation
I. INTRODUCTION
Modern technologies have opened a world of possibilities to
our society. Among them, assisted living has proven useful to
ease the frailty that comes with aging. By means of cameras,
sensors, and actuators, many aspects of living can be moni-
tored, evaluated, and enhanced. The use of cameras in private
homes is still something of a new practice, whereas in nursing
homes and assisted living facilities for seniors they are more
commonly found, especially to monitor trafﬁc in common
rooms and entrances. The oldest segment of population, aged
80 and over, particularly prone to falls and its consequences
is the fastest growing within older population expected to
The authors would like to thank Fundac¸˜ao Cearense de Apoio ao De-
senvolvimento Cient´ıﬁco e Tecnol´ogico (FUNCAP) and the University of
Fortaleza (UNIFOR) for their ﬁnancial support.

in the code is paid through Copyright Clearance Center, 222 Rosewood

IEEE Copyrights Manager at pubs-permissions@ieee.org. All rights reserved.
Copyright ©2020 by IEEE.
represent 20% of senior population by 2050 [1]. Thus, within
this context, the detection and prevention of accidents are
paramount to ensure elder care and a longer life expectancy,
providing more opportunities to elderly people to attain a
higher quality of life.
In particular, among incidents, falls are the leading source
of injuries which degrade health condition. The correlation
between falls and early death is notorious. Hence, fall de-
tection solutions play a vital role in reducing the number of
complications and comorbidities, respectively, derived from
and associated with falling.
Over the years, many approaches for fall detection have
been proposed. Sensors are, by far, the most common ap-
proach, being them wearable or strategically placed within the
monitored environment. However, these approaches often fail
in non-technical ways, such as users forgetting to wear their
devices or falling on untracked territory [2]. A more sensitive
approach is to rely on cameras, as they are easier to setup
and consistently provide data. Additionally, cameras can more
easily track the entire environment than sensors. Nevertheless,
this also comes with its own costs, as cameras provide only
indirect monitoring, requiring, often complex, computer vision
software to extract relevant features.
A recent approach is to use pose estimation software to iden-
tify humans and pinpoint the location of their most important
joints [3]–[5], such as shoulders, elbows, and knees [6]. This
pose information can be later used as features for an effective
fall detection system. In a sense, this technique bridges the
gap between body-worn sensors and camera-based monitoring,
providing the information of the former, using data from the
latter. Moreover, this technique requires no special hardware,
such as depth cameras, making it a compelling alternative to
current solutions.
This scenario begs the question: Can pose estimation meth-
ods be as effective as the sensor or other camera-based ones?
To answer this question, we analyzed three pose estimation
frameworks: OpenPose [7], AlphaPose [8], and PoseNet [9],
totalizing eleven models, paired with a simple neural network
classiﬁer. In our experiments, we have obtained competitive
results among the state-of-the-art on the UR Fall Detection
dataset, a multi-modal fall detection benchmark, comprised of
RGB, depth, and acceleration data, captured through two Mi-
Authorized licensed use limited to: Universidade de Fortaleza. Downloaded on September 30,2020 at 15:50:37 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 
978-1-7281-9042-6/20/$31.00 ©2020 IEEE


Accelera on
Time
Figure 1. The UR Fall Detection dataset [11] and its modes:
RGB, Depth, and Acceleration.
crosoft Kinetic [10] devices and a waist-mounted accelerom-
eter, as shown in Figure 1. More speciﬁcally, our best model
achieved a sensitivity rate of 94.5% and a speciﬁcity rate of
99.9%, in line with the best camera and sensor-based solutions.
II. RELATED WORK
A closer look to most early systems and current work on
fall detection reveals that, conceptually, they solve the problem
the same way: the RGB/Depth/Acceleration data undergoes
feature extraction, an optional ﬁltering/denoising is performed,
and a ﬁnal classiﬁer is attached at the end [2]–[5], [12]–[21].
The most popular feature extraction methods for image data
are simple visual descriptors obtained by image processing
[2], [12], [13] and CNNs [3]–[5], [14]–[16] and, for the
acceleration data, are simple thresholds [17]–[20]. Several
forms of averaging and ﬁltering can be used to improve the
system robustness to noise in feature domain [13]. Finally,
most works employ popular classiﬁers, such as, SVM [2],
[12], [13], [17] and LSTM [21]. SVMs are popular due to
their simplicity, and LSTMs for their effectiveness in dealing
with time-series. To a lesser extent, simple neural networks
are used for the classiﬁcation, typically associated with a CNN
feature extractor [14].
Using acceleration data only, Theodoris et al. [18] and
Santos et al. [19] explore neural networks applied to the tri-
axial acceleration data. Both works evaluate the acceleration
time-series as-is, the former using a LSTM model and the
latter a 1D CNN. In addition, these works also describe the
need for data augmentation to improve the system reliability.
Inspired by the computer vision literature, they experiment
with random rotations and mirroring, mimicking the collection
of acceleration data for falls in all directions.
A number of existing studies in the literature have examined
the use of wearables in isolation, however, despite the results
presented so far, these devices are still regarded as prone
to false-alarming [2]. Using the available RGB and Depth
images, some authors have developed hybrid approaches [17],
[20]. In a nutshell, if the acceleration data crosses some
threshold, a warning is issued, triggering an image-based sub-
system. This two-part approach saves considerable resources
by activating the image-based detection only when needed.
The work of Kepsky & Kwolek [17] uses a SVM model
for the classiﬁcation and a buffer of depth images captured
from the ceiling mounted Kinetic device to extract height,
width, and area descriptors. Jansi & Amutha [20] follow a
similar approach, but they propose a novel descriptor: Entropy
of Depth Difference Gradient Map, which is fed to a sparse
representation-based classiﬁer.
Using traditional image processing algorithms, several
works extract subjects from raw footage using background
removal techniques [12], [13], [22]. This is especially effective
because surveillance cameras are static, easing the background
removal task considerably. Using the foreground silhouette,
several features can be extracted, such as the person’s centroid,
width, height, and bounding box [12]. Ellipsoid ﬁtting has
been also employed to derive width-to-height ratios and orien-
tation [22]. The overall shape and pose can also be summarized
using area-based methods, as shown in the work of Harrou et
al. [13], which shows the effectiveness of using exponential
moving averages to smooth features, improving the overall
quality of the descriptors. While these works use RGB data
for the feature extraction, Mohd et al. [2] shows this is also
feasible using the depth footage.
Based on the growing deep-learning literature, many ap-
proaches employ the raw images as inputs, performing little
to no feature engineering [14]–[16]. Nunez-Marcos et al. [14]\napply the TVL-1 algorithm to extract optical ﬂow images and
a convolutional neural network to perform the classiﬁcation.
Utilizing raw images, Xiaogang et al. [15] and Cai et al.
[16] have proposed different CNN architectures to classify
frames as “falls” or “no falls”. Using more than one image
per prediction, Abobakr et al. apply a CNN to extract features
from frames and a LSTM model to aggregate the temporal
evolution, yielding a more accurate prediction [21].
Instead of general-purpose CNN extractors, a limited set
of authors experiment with pose estimation models as an
specialized tool to acquire semantic rich features for fall
detection and, generally, activity recognition [3]–[5]. These
networks are pre-trained on large datasets, such as the MPI
[6] and [23], to identify the main keypoints of the human
body. Using pose estimation, experiments have been done
using SVMs [3] or simple thresholds [5]. Given extra data,
such as stereo-cameras, 3D poses can be estimated, providing
additional context for classiﬁcation [4]. Unfortunately, none of
these works present results on public datasets, rendering any
Authorized licensed use limited to: Universidade de Fortaleza. Downloaded on September 30,2020 at 15:50:37 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 


Figure 2. Overview of our solution. Individual frames are processed by the pose estimation model, followed by the neural
network classiﬁer, which classiﬁes the pose as a “fall” (red) or as a “no fall” (green).
comparative statements speculative at best.
In this work, we follow the idea of feeding the entire
image to a neural network, however, to a general purpose
pose estimation model [7]–[9], particularly trained on key
point detection datasets [6], [23]. This produces a feature set
that explicitly describes the person pose and can be handed
over to a classiﬁcation model, such as a KNN, SVM or
neural network. In our case, we use a simple multi-layer
neural network model using batch normalization and the ReLU
activation function. Finally, we also evaluate our system under
the UR Fall Detection dataset [11], similarly as done in all
previous research covered in this section, therefore, allowing
for a direct comparison.
III. FALL DETECTION USING POSE ESTIMATION
In this section we continue by outlining our solution. To
evaluate the potential of pose estimation as a set of features
for fall detection, we consider a simple model based on a fully
connected neural network and three pose estimation systems:
OpenPose [7], AlphaPose [8], and PoseNet [9]. At a high level,
the raw video frames are fed to the pose estimation system,
which outputs a (potentially empty) set of poses. If at least
one pose is detected, we feed it to the neural network, which
produces a binary fall/no fall answer. This process is depicted
in Figure 2.
The three aforementioned pose estimation solutions we have
selected are state-of-the-art in either quality or speed. The
OpenPose [7] system features three models trained on the
MPI [6] and COCO [23] datasets, being the third trained
on an extended version of COCO, referred to as Body25.\nThese models focus on detection quality and are so far among
the best models available in the literature. Similarly, the
AlphaPose [8] framework features six models based around
different neural network architectures, all focused on detection
quality and trained on the COCO dataset. Finally, PoseNet [9],
focuses on speed, being suitable for real time performance

when executing on desktops and smartphones. It has two
variants, one based on the MobileNet architecture and other
based on a ResNet50, posing a trade-off between quality and
speed. Table I shows a breakdown of these three systems.
Collectively, these systems are fed a 640 × 480 image,
which is resized to ﬁt the network inputs. Then, they output
3 · K values, being K the number of key points detected
by the model, with 15 ≤K ≤25. The OpenPose MPI,
COCO and Body25 models output, respectively, 15, 18 and
25 key points. In particular, all AlphaPose models return
18, while all PoseNet models return 17. For each joint, its
three values correspond to the joint (x, y) coordinates and the
network’s conﬁdence c, representing how correctly it estimated
the key point. In sequence, the predicted pose is fed to a
fully-connected neural network. Each layer of the network
consists of n neurons, followed by the batch normalization
operation and the ReLU activation function [24], [25]. Each
neuron of a layer deﬁnes an afﬁne transformation of its inputs
x. Considering all neurons at once, this can be deﬁned in
Authorized licensed use limited to: Universidade de Fortaleza. Downloaded on September 30,2020 at 15:50:37 UTC from IEEE Xplore.  Restrictions apply. 


matrix notation as follows:
y = W tx + b
where W, x and b are, respectively, the weight vector, input
vector and bias.
The batch normalization operation estimates the mean and
standard deviation of each neuron and standardizes it to have
zero mean and unit variance. This operation is used to stabilize
training, as it constrains all neurons to produce outputs in
a stable range. After it, a learnable afﬁne transformation is
applied to each neuron independently. The complete batch
normalization operation is deﬁned as follows:
BN(x) = γ x −µ
σ2
+ β
Being µ and σ2 the estimated mean and standard deviation
of each neuron and γ and β the afﬁne transformation. In
this equation, all elements are vectors and all multiplications
are carried element-wise. Finally, the result of the batch
normalization is passed through the ReLU activation function,
expressed as:
ReLU(x) = max(0, x)
This function has the sole purpose of making the ﬁnal
outputs of the layer non-linear. Thus, making each layer
numerically independent of the others.
The ﬁnal output of the network is a layer with a single
neuron passed through the sigmoid activation function, instead
of the batch normalization and ReLU pair. This function
squishes the input values to outputs between [0, 1], herein
interpreted as: 0 as “no fall” and 1 as “fall”. Any value
in between this interval can be understood as the network
conﬁdence towards one or the other. The sigmoid function
can be deﬁned as follows:
Sigmoid(x) =
ex
1 + ex
Finally, considering the poses available for training and their
respective labels, the network is trained using gradient descent
to minimize the cross-entropy between the correct labels and
the model’s predictions [24]. This is equivalent to maximize
the log-likelihood of the model. Thus, we focus on minimizing
the following expression:
−ylog(ˆy) −(1 −y)log(1 −ˆy)
where y is the pose’s correct label (0 or 1) and ˆy is the
model prediction for the same pose.
IV. EVALUATION METRICS
The UR Fall detection dataset contains 30 videos of falls and
40 videos of activities of daily living (ADL). This sums up to
11, 936 frames, out of which 7, 452 are labelled as “no falls”,
2, 373 as “falls”, and the remaining 1, 719 are transitioning
frames. As done in the original work in which the particular
dataset was presented [11], we consider only the “fall” and
“no fall” frames, discarding all transitions. In the following,
we present the metrics we have used for the pose estimation
and classiﬁer:
A. Pose Estimation Metrics
Average Precision (AP): Pose estimation systems are com-
pared among each other using the Average Precision score
[26]. This metric is computed by the framework developers on
the datasets used to train the pose estimator, such as COCO
and MPI [6], [23], that is, not on the current dataset. At a high
level, it evaluates how close the detected joints are from the
ground truth joints, measuring how similar the detected pose
is to the actual pose. The metric is weighted to account for
the difﬁculty in detecting each key point and is regarded as an
effective indicator of the detection quality. Unfortunately, we
were unable to get these values about the PoseNet framework,
as its authors did not provide this information.
Frame Accuracy (FA): Speciﬁcally to the UR Fall detec-
tion dataset, there are no ground truth poses, which means
that the AP scores can not be computed for it. However, we
can still have a rough sense of the estimation accuracy by
considering how often the detectors could locate a person,
given that there is a person to be detected. Out of the 11, 936
total frames in which a person is present, 11, 544 contain a
person in its foreground. Thus, the detection accuracy can be
stated as n/11, 544 being n the number of frames in which a
person was detected.
Key Point Accuracy (KPA): To evaluate the detection
quality at the key point level, we can leverage the detectors
per-joint conﬁdence scores. The OpenPose framework uses
60% as its conﬁdence cut-off point. Any joint detected with
less than 60% accuracy is considered a bad detection and
is omitted from the results. Borrowing this cut-off point,
we can compute the average number of detected joints for
each detector by counting how many joints were detected
with conﬁdence above 60%. Thus, the optimal value for each
model would be 15, 18 and 25, respectively. Comparing the
unnormalized values for this metric, we are able to compare
how often the extra joints of the COCO and Body25 are
effectively detected when compared to the MPI model.
The Average Precision, Frame Accuracy and Key Point
Accuracy scores for each detector are shown in Table II.

B. Classiﬁer Metrics
The output of the model is a value between 0 and 1. Using
a threshold T, this value can be converted to “no fall” if < T
and as a “fall” if > T. With this binarization scheme, we can
compute the following four quantities:
1) True Positives: “fall” poses detected as falls.
2) False Positives: “no fall” poses detected as falls.
3) True Negatives: “no fall” poses classiﬁed as such.
4) False Negatives: “fall” poses classiﬁed as “no falls”.
From these quantities, we can compute the sensitivity and
speciﬁcity of the model [27]. The former measures how well
the model detects “falls”, while the latter measures how well
the model detects “no falls”. Informally, they measure the
probability of detection and the resiliency to false-alarms.
When both measures are high, it can be said that the model
detects falls and only falls. Both rates can be computed as:
Sensitivity =
True Positives
True Positives + False Negatives
Specificity =
True Negatives
True Negatives + False Positives
By tuning the threshold T, the sensitivity or speciﬁcity
can be favored over the other. A low T value renders the
model more sensitive, but less speciﬁc, while a high T does
the opposite. Plotting the model sensitivity over the inverse
speciﬁcity yields the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve
(ROC) [24], which shows the trade-off obtained by tuning T.
This trade-off can be further summarized by taking the area
under the ROC curve (AUC). This metric effectively sums up
the quality of the model in a single value, suitable for model
comparison. It ranges from 0.5, which is equivalent to random
guessing, to 1, the perfect model.
Finally, while the AUC metric acts as a quality summary,
we still need to pick a T value to compute the model best
sensitivity and speciﬁcity pair. For that, some other metric
is necessary. In this work, we particularly search for the T
value that maximizes the geometric mean of the sensitivity
and speciﬁcity, that is, the T value that maximizes:
p
sensitivity · specificity
The geometric mean has the nice property of requiring
that both metrics be high to attain a high score, i.e., it
penalizes more the case in which one metric is signiﬁcantly
better/worse than the other. Thus, it is a more neutral score
than the traditional arithmetic mean, striving to maximize both
while keeping them relatively well balanced. In this work, all
sensitivity and speciﬁcity scores are derived by ﬁnding the T
value which maximizes the score. The search is conducted by
a simple grid search.
C. Training Settings
We trained our model for each one of the eleven pose detec-
tion models for 100 epochs using a batch-size of 1, 024 poses
and the Nadam [28] optimizer, conﬁgured with a learning rate
of 0.0005. In each layer, 128 neurons were used. Everything
else is set to the default settings of TensorFlow 2.1.
For a fair and reproducible evaluation, we split the dataset
into a training and validation set, being the latter composed
of every fourth video. Thus, it is composed of approximately
25% of the available data under the same distribution. Splitting
by video (instead of by frame) also ensures that the model is
exposed to novel sequences during validation. Additionally, we
only consider the ﬁrst Kinect camera, which is front facing,
as it correlates better with the expected camera placement in
typical surveillance settings.
V. RESULTS
We begin our analysis by considering how many layers our
neural network needs to achieve its peak performance. Table
III shows detailed performance when the neural network is
trained for each pose detection model, varying the number
of layers L used for the classiﬁcation. For L = 0, the
model degenerates into a simple logistic regression classiﬁer,
a baseline. For L = 1 and L = 2, most solutions reach their
peak AUC scores. For L > 2, the model starts to over-ﬁt the
problem, degrading its performance. Due to the already high
AUC scores, we chose not to explore changing the number of
neurons per layer, as this would only add complexity to our
model. Table III also shows the sensitivity and speciﬁcity of
the best performing model of each row. In general, these scores
are considered high, as they are all above the 90% mark, with
only a few exceptions.
Regarding which model performs best, all models showed
excellent
numbers,
making
this
a
difﬁcult
comparison.
Framework-wise, the OpenPose is the least performing, fol-
lowed by a draw between the AlphaPose and PoseNet frame-
works: both frameworks achieve a 0.995 AUC score. However,
we argue that AlphaPose is more precise than PoseNet, as
Table II shows that it detects, on average, 15.66 joints per
pose, versus 10.33. On the other hand, the PoseNet framework
is more efﬁcient, achieving 24 FPS versus 5.34, as shown
in Table I. For the fall detection problem, we believe the
efﬁciency is more relevant, as a real world application would
require several cameras being monitored simultaneously, thus,
the efﬁciency could greatly reduce costs.
At this point, it is relevant to investigate which aspects
of the pose estimators possess predictive power over the fall
detection performance. In other words, if there is any property
of the estimator that correlates with a better AUC score for
the network. To evaluate this, we plot the best AUC scores of
each model over the AP, FA and KPA scores of each detector,
presented in Table II. This comparison is shown in Figure
3. In each plot, a difﬁcult correlation analysis is presented.
For OpenPose, counter-intuitively, the found correlation is
negative, as the MPI model has the worst statistics but the best
results. The same can be seen for PoseNet models, although it
provides only two instances, limiting our analysis. Looking at
the AlphaPose data, a more intuitive picture is shown: better
estimators yielded better detectors. The correlation is clearer
in the key point accuracy plot. Overall, this analysis shows that

the relationship between the pose estimator and the classiﬁer
model is not a trivial one.
Finally, we compare our best results, obtained with the
AlphaPose + ResNet152 and PoseNet + MobileNet, with the
state-of-the-art presented in Section 2, which comprises works
using the acceleration, depth and RGB data. Table IV shows
the best sensitivity and speciﬁcity pairs reported for each
work, as well as their modality, used features and classiﬁcation
method. In general, all methods obtained near perfect scores,
with most works reaching near 100% on one of the two
metrics, including ours. Considering that our approach uses
a single pose for its prediction and simple neural network
architecture, we argue that pose estimation is a promising
general-purpose feature set for action recognition.
VI. CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORK
This work focused on the use of pose estimation techniques
applied to the domain of fall detection algorithms. In more
detail, we explored the use of the OpenPose, AlphaPose, and
PoseNet frameworks associated with a simple neural network
classiﬁer to classify video frames as having falls or no falls,
achieving a top sensitivity rate of 94.5% and speciﬁcity rate
of 99.9%. In total, we have tested eleven pose estimation
models for six network sizes, uncovering that single and two
layered networks provide the best performance. However, we
could not ﬁnd a clear correlation between the pose estimation
model metrics and the associated classiﬁer results. Finally,
we compared our results to those reported by several authors
using a myriad of techniques and input sources. This analysis
showed that, although simple, our solutions are competitive,
demonstrating that pose estimation is a promising research
area for fall detection and, in general, activity recognition.
While we obtained promising results with our models, it
is worth highlighting that this work is still preliminary in the
broad investigation of fall detection. As for any system with
potential use in health, large-scale trials are needed to properly
assess the system behaviour under real circumstances. More
speciﬁcally, several practical aspects have yet to be studied for
a clearer picture of pose estimation as a tool for fall detection.
Among such aspects, the problem of low-light and occlusion
are worth further investigation, as well as the role of multiple
cameras and their proper placement.
On the technical side, many improvements to neural net-
works have been proposed over the years and could potentially
improve the results obtained in this work. Additionally, our
current analysis is limited to a single frame per prediction.
As done in other previous works [18], [21], leveraging several
frames using LSTM units might yield improvements to both
